Details: Direct filtration is expected to remove V. cholerae based on data for similar microbiological contaminants, such as E. coli or heterotrophic bacteria; however, few data specific to V. cholerae are available [1678]. The results of one study investigating the removal of V. cholerae attached to plankton using cloth, such as Nylon net or cotton Sari worn by women, as filter material suggest more than 95 percent removal of V. cholerae by filtration only (i.e. no other treatment) [1684].  A second, similar study in Bangledesh reported a 48 percent reduction in cholera infections by using the same simple filtration method [1685].
